does: This paper develops an automated pipeline to generate and evaluate natural language explanations for the millions of latent features discovered by sparse autoencoders applied to large language models. The authors use LLMs themselves to produce explanations for SAE features and introduce five new scoring techniques to assess explanation quality, including a novel intervention scoring method that measures the interpretability of effects when features are artificially activated or deactivated. They validate their framework across SAEs of different architectures, activation functions, and loss functions trained on multiple open-weight models, and provide evidence that intervention-based evaluation identifies interpretable features that traditional recall-based metrics miss.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature using sparse autoencoders to decompose neural network activations into interpretable components, extending earlier efforts to make neural network internals more transparent. It responds to the scalability problem in mechanistic interpretability: while prior work showed SAEs can reveal interpretable features compared to individual neurons, the resulting feature spaces contain too many dimensions for manual analysis. The paper connects to recent developments in using language models as tools for AI interpretability and explanation generation, drawing on methods from feature importance literature while introducing intervention-based evaluation inspired by causal inference approaches in neuroscience and mechanistic interpretability.
enables: This work opens pathways toward scalable, automated interpretability of large language models by providing the infrastructure to understand millions of learned features simultaneously. The proposed explanation techniques and evaluation metrics establish baselines for automated feature interpretation that could accelerate discovery of unexpected behaviors, safety-relevant patterns, or failure modes in LLMs. The intervention scoring method in particular enables new forms of analysis of feature causality and could support future work on targeted model editing, adversarial robustness analysis, and understanding emergent capabilities. The open-source pipeline facilitates broader adoption of mechanistic interpretability research and could feed into AI safety efforts requiring detailed understanding of model internals.
